# Bronchoalveolar lavage in drug-induced lung disease

Ulrich Costabel, MD, FCCPa,*, Esra Uzaslan, MDᵇ, Josune Guzman, MDᶜ

aRuhrlandklinik, Department Pneumology/Allergy, Tueschener Weg 40, D-45239 Essen, Germany

bUludag Universitesi, Tip Faku  Go                            Hast. AD, Go  16059, Bursa, Turkey

cAllgemeine und Experimentelle Pathologie, Ruhr-Universita    Universita         150, D-4780 Bochum, Germany

Since its first application in the 1970s [1] as a valu-       such as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, sarcoidosis, and hypersensitivity pneumonitis [10 – 14].

This article reviews some technical aspects of BAL, gives an overview on the use of BAL in the differential diagnosis of interstitial lung disease, and concludes with a detailed description of BAL findings in drug-induced lung disease.

# Technical aspects

Guidelines and recommendations for a standardized approach to the performance of BAL and the laboratory processing and the analysis of recovered fluid have been published [4,9,15,16]. When the guidelines are followed, the results of lavage are valid for practical diagnostic purposes.

# Bronchoscopic procedure

BAL usually is performed during fiberoptic bronchoscopy with topical anesthesia following general inspection of the tracheobronchial tree. The fiberoptic bronchoscope is introduced gently until impacted, or “wedged,” in a segmental or subsegmental bronchus. In diffuse lung disease, the middle or lingular lobe is recommended as a standard site of BAL. Localized disease requires lavage of the radiographically-involved area.

Commonly, sterile, unbuffered isotonic saline (0.9% NaCl solution) is used as the instillate. The fluid is instilled with syringes through the biopsy channel of the bronchoscope, using multiple aliquots of 20 mL to 60 mL (four to five aliquots are the most common), to a total volume of 100 mL to 300 mL. Smaller instilled volumes (less than 100 mL) increase the likelihood of contamination of lavage.

# U. Costabel et al / Clin Chest Med 25 (2004) 25–35

Fluid by the bronchial spaces, including inflammatory cells from this compartment, which may influence the differential cell counts [17]. After each instillation, the aliquot is recovered immediately by gentle hand suction into the syringe or gentle wall suction into a fluid trap. Usually, 40% to 70% of the instilled volume is recovered.

BAL generally is well tolerated. Side effects are similar to those of routine fiberoptic bronchoscopy under local anesthesia. There is practically no mortality and the complication rate ranges between 0% and 2.3%, compared with 7% with transbronchial biopsy and 13% with surgical lung biopsy [18,19]. There are no absolute contraindications for performance of BAL beyond those noted for bronchoscopy.

| Macrophages | greater than 80% |
| ----------- | ---------------- |
| Lymphocytes | up to 15%        |
| Neutrophils | up to 3%         |
| Eosinophils | up to 0.5%       |
| Mast cells  | up to 0.5%       |

The total recovered fluid should be transferred to the laboratory as quickly as possible because the cells are not well-preserved in the saline solution [20]. The total number of cells is counted in a hemocytometer, either in a sample of the pooled native fluid or in a resuspension of the cells after the first centrifugation. Cell viability is assessed by trypan blue exclusion and should range from 80% to 95%.

For the enumeration of cell differentials, at least 600 cells are counted after applying May-Grünwald-Giemsa stain. A high percentage of epithelial cells (> 5%) is indicative of contamination of the alveolar samples by bronchial cells. Such BAL probes may not be representative for the diagnosis of diffuse parenchymal lung disease. Several soluble components have been measured in BAL fluid. None of them has proven to be useful in clinical settings [21].

# Bronchoalveolar lavage as a clinical diagnostic tool

BAL is indicated in every patient who has unclear interstitial lung disease or unclear pulmonary shadowing, no matter what cause is suspected. The underlying disorders may be of infectious, noninfectious, immunologic, or malignant origin. BAL also may be indicated in patients who have normal chest radiograms when clinical and lung function tests are abnormal and point toward a diffuse lung disease or in patients who have unexplained pulmonary symptoms and in whom a normal BAL finding may allow the exclusion of significant, active interstitial lung disease.

# Interpretation of bronchoalveolar lavage cytology

Changes in the profile of the cell differentials of BAL fluid have been described in various interstitial lung diseases. Clearly, an abnormal BAL cell differential count does not allow a specific diagnosis, as it does with conventional differential blood counts; however, BAL studies should not be limited to counting the cell differentials. At least as important as examining cell differentials is to note the morphologic appearances of cells and particles. Examples are the different morphology in hypersensitivity pneumonitis (foamy macrophages, heterogeneous macrophage size, presence of plasma cells) versus that of sarcoidosis (more monomorphous appearance of macrophages, less activated lymphocytes), the presence of malignant cells, the characteristic features of alveolar proteinosis, or the detection of dust particles, such as asbestos bodies in occupational exposure conditions [24].

Furthermore, BAL cell differentials should not be used as an isolated finding for making a diagnosis but always should be interpreted in the context of disease history and clinical, laboratory, and radiologic findings.

# Bronchoalveolar lavage in differential diagnosis of interstitial lung diseases

BAL has improved the diagnostic work-up; some investigators use BAL more frequently than transbronchial lung biopsy. In two recently-published international statements on the major interstitial lung diseases, BAL was considered to be helpful in strengthening the diagnosis in patients who had sarcoidosis in the absence of biopsy [25]; BAL or transbronchial biopsy were considered requirements for the exclusion of other diseases in a patient who had idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) who did not undergo surgical biopsy (one of the four major criteria for making a clinical diagnosis of the disease) [26].

BAL findings may, on occasion, be extremely specific, so that they can directly confirm a particular diagnosis and replace lung biopsy (Table 1). Many of these disorders are included in the group of the alveolar filling syndromes. Obviously, the abnormal material that accumulates in the alveolar spaces in these syndromes can be washed out easily by lavage and is so characteristic that a specific BAL diagnosis is possible.

| BAL finding                                                                                                       | Diagnosis                              |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Pneumocystis carinii, fungi, cytomegalovirus transformed cells                                                    | Opportunistic infections               |
| Milky effluent, periodic acid-schiff positive noncellular corpuscles, amorphous debris, foamy macrophages         | Alveolar proteinosis                   |
| Hemosiderin-laden macrophages, intracytoplasmic fragments of red blood cells in macrophages, free red blood cells | Alveolar hemorrhage syndrome           |
| Malignant cells of solid tumors, lymphoma, leukemia                                                               | Malignant infiltrates                  |
| Dust particles in macrophages, quantifying asbestos bodies                                                        | Dust exposure                          |
| Eosinophils greater than 25%                                                                                      | Eosinophilic lung disease              |
| Positive lymphocyte transformation test to beryllium                                                              | Chronic beryllium disease              |
| CD1 positive Langerhans cells increased                                                                           | Langerhans cell histiocytosis          |
| Atypical hyperplastic type II pneumocytes                                                                         | Diffuse alveolar damage, drug toxicity |

Data from Costabel U. Atlas of bronchoalveolar lavage. London: Chapman and Hall; 1998.

In other selected lung diseases, BAL findings are not diagnostic but may help narrow the differential diagnosis in the appropriate clinical setting if the BAL results fit with the clinician’s suspicion and with the imaging results. The list of diseases that have a lymphocytic, neutrophilic, eosinophilic, or mixed cellular pattern is long (Table 2).

items.md: BAL has improved the diagnostic work-up; some investigators use BAL more frequently than transbronchial lung biopsy. In two recently-published international statements on the major interstitial lung diseases, BAL was considered to be helpful in strengthening the diagnosis in patients who had sarcoidosis in the absence of biopsy [25]; BAL or transbronchial biopsy were considered requirements for the exclusion of other diseases in a patient who had idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) who did not undergo surgical biopsy (one of the four major criteria for making a clinical diagnosis of the disease) [26].

items.md: BAL findings may, on occasion, be extremely specific, so that they can directly confirm a particular diagnosis and replace lung biopsy (Table 1). Many of these disorders are included in the group of the alveolar filling syndromes. Obviously, the abnormal material that accumulates in the alveolar spaces in these syndromes can be washed out easily by lavage and is so characteristic that a specific BAL diagnosis is possible.

increase in lymphocytes can be differentiated further [32]. Table 4 lists, in detail, the changes in BAL cytology that has been reported for individual drug. Amiodarone causes characteristic changes in the alveolar macrophage population, which shows foamy intracytoplasmic alterations and corresponds to a form of phospholipidosis [27,28]. The drug induces an increase in the phospholipid content of all body cells, and, especially, phagocytic cells. Electron microscopy shows that the vacuoles in the cytoplasm correspond to large numbers of surfactant-like lamellar bodies, which is consistent with the phospholipid nature of these inclusions. This feature also is seen in patients who are treated with amiodarone but are free of clinical lung involvement. If foamy macrophages are not present in BAL, an amiodarone-induced pneumonitis likely can be excluded. Conversely, a normal BAL differential does not exclude amiodarone-induced lung disease [37 – 39].

# Assessment of disease activity

BAL findings are not, however, specific for any drug-induced lung disease; the definitive diagnosis needs additional clinical investigations. BAL is useful, however, to exclude other pulmonary problems that commonly are associated with chemotherapeutic agents.

# Bronchoalveolar lavage in drug-induced pneumonitis

Many drugs can induce a toxic or immune-mediated interstitial lung reaction. In addition to toxic changes of the type II pneumocytes, any type of alveolitis (lymphocytic, neutrophilic, eosinophilic, or mixed) and diffuse alveolar hemorrhage may be present in BAL (Box 1) [4,24,27 – 29]. The most frequent finding is a lymphocytic alveolitis with the predominance of CD8⁺ cells, just as in extrinsic allergic alveolitis. In methotrexate-induced pneumonitis, the CD4⁺ cells may be increased preferentially [30,31]; however another study reported the opposite.

# Diagnostic features of drug-induced lung disease

The diagnosis of drug-induced lung disease usually is based on several criteria [103], including:

- A history of drug exposure
- Clinical, imaging, and histopathologic patterns that are consistent with earlier observations with the same drug
- Exclusion of other lung disease
- Improvement following discontinuation of the suspected drug
- Recurrence of symptoms on rechallenge (this may be done in certain circumstances, but usually is considered unethical in drugs that cause irreversible pulmonary changes) [103]

BAL may contribute to several of these criteria.

Box 1. Overview on BAL in drug-induced interstitial lung disease. For a detailed listing see Table 4.

# Lymphocytosis

- Azathioprine

# Neutrophilia

- Bleomycin
- Busulfan

# Eosinophilia

- Bleomycin
- Nitrofurantoin

# Haemorrhage

# Cytotoxic reaction

- Bleomycin

- Nitrosureas
- Busulfan
- Cyclophosphamide

Bronchoalveolar lavage to support a certain type of drug-induced lung disease. BAL may support a certain clinical-pathologic pattern of lung involvement:

Cellular pneumonitis, also called “hypersensitivity pneumonitis,” is the most common drug-induced interstitial lung disease. BAL shows the characteristic pattern with an isolated and marked increase in lymphocytes, usually greater than 50%. A moderate increase in neutrophils and other cells also may be present. Usually, the CD4:CD8 ratio is low [33,46,82,87,93].

Pulmonary infiltrates with eosinophilia or eosinophilic pneumonia is a reaction that is associated with many drugs. BAL shows significant eosinophilia [33]. The absence of BAL eosinophilia is rather exclusive of pulmonary eosinophilic infiltrates.

Organizing pneumonia is another pattern that is associated with several drugs. BAL usually shows a mixed pattern with increases in lymphocytes, neutrophils, eosinophils, and mast cells [104]. Foamy alveolar macrophages and a small number of plasma cells also can be seen. The CD4:CD8 ratio is low.

A cytotoxic reaction, associated with cytostatic chemotherapy, may show histologic changes, such as hyaline membranes, atypia and hyperplasia of type II pneumocytes, and diffuse fibroblast proliferation (i.e., a similar pattern to diffuse alveolar damage). BAL may show clusters of atypical type II pneumocytes and a marked increase in neutrophils; this is seen commonly in patients who have acute respiratory distress syndrome or acute interstitial pneumonia [105].

Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage is associated with a few drugs. BAL is the ideal technique to demonstrate the hemosiderin-laden alveolar macrophages, in addition to the presence of free red blood cells.

The major role of BAL is to exclude infections; this is essential in patients who have acute febrile.

items.md: Bronchoalveolar lavage to support a certain type of drug-induced lung disease. BAL may support a certain clinical-pathologic pattern of lung involvement:
items.value: Bronchoalveolar lavage to support a certain type of drug-induced lung disease. BAL may support a certain clinical-pathologic pattern of lung involvement:

items.md: Cellular pneumonitis, also called “hypersensitivity pneumonitis,” is the most common drug-induced interstitial lung disease. BAL shows the characteristic pattern with an isolated and marked increase in lymphocytes, usually greater than 50%. A moderate increase in neutrophils and other cells also may be present. Usually, the CD4:CD8 ratio is low [33,46,82,87,93].
items.value: Cellular pneumonitis, also called “hypersensitivity pneumonitis,” is the most common drug-induced interstitial lung disease. BAL shows the characteristic pattern with an isolated and marked increase in lymphocytes, usually greater than 50%. A moderate increase in neutrophils and other cells also may be present. Usually, the CD4:CD8 ratio is low [33,46,82,87,93].

items.md: Pulmonary infiltrates with eosinophilia or eosinophilic pneumonia is a reaction that is associated with many drugs. BAL shows significant eosinophilia [33]. The absence of BAL eosinophilia is rather exclusive of pulmonary eosinophilic infiltrates.
items.value: Pulmonary infiltrates with eosinophilia or eosinophilic pneumonia is a reaction that is associated with many drugs. BAL shows significant eosinophilia [33]. The absence of BAL eosinophilia is rather exclusive of pulmonary eosinophilic infiltrates.

items.md: Organizing pneumonia is another pattern that is associated with several drugs. BAL usually shows a mixed pattern with increases in lymphocytes, neutrophils, eosinophils, and mast cells [104]. Foamy alveolar macrophages and a small number of plasma cells also can be seen. The CD4:CD8 ratio is low.
items.value: Organizing pneumonia is another pattern that is associated with several drugs. BAL usually shows a mixed pattern with increases in lymphocytes, neutrophils, eosinophils, and mast cells [104]. Foamy alveolar macrophages and a small number of plasma cells also can be seen. The CD4:CD8 ratio is low.

items.md: A cytotoxic reaction, associated with cytostatic chemotherapy, may show histologic changes, such as hyaline membranes, atypia and hyperplasia of type II pneumocytes, and diffuse fibroblast proliferation (i.e., a similar pattern to diffuse alveolar damage). BAL may show clusters of atypical type II pneumocytes and a marked increase in neutrophils; this is seen commonly in patients who have acute respiratory distress syndrome or acute interstitial pneumonia [105].

items.md: Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage is associated with a few drugs. BAL is the ideal technique to demonstrate the hemosiderin-laden alveolar macrophages, in addition to the presence of free red blood cells.

a Foamy alveolar macrophages.

Interstitial lung disease and in those who receive immunosuppressive therapy. It is important to exclude opportunistic infections, because methotrexate, corticosteroids, immunosuppressives, and infliximab have been associated with the development of bacterial, fungal, parasitic (including Pneumocystis carinii), and viral infections. BAL has a high sensitivity and specificity (>95%) to diagnose P carinii pneumonia. Globally, the sensitivity of BAL in the diagnosis of bacterial pneumonia is 60% to 90%; it is 70% to 80% in mycobacterial, fungal, and viral infections.

Many cardiovascular drugs have been reported to induce interstitial lung disease. The differential diagnosis of left heart failure with pulmonary congestion is a frequent consideration. The diagnosis of pulmonary congestion due to left heart failure can be supported by the BAL finding of diffuse alveolar hemorrhage. It is not known that cardiovascular drugs can induce diffuse alveolar hemorrhage (except for anticoagulants). Conversely, if the BAL cytology is normal it largely can exclude a drug-induced immunologic reaction (eg, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, organizing pneumonia, eosinophilic lung disease).

# Bronchoalveolar lavage in the diagnosis of underlying disorders

Pulmonary involvement by a malignancy or a systemic disease, such as connective tissue disorders and inflammatory bowel disease, is important. BAL can contribute to the diagnosis of metastatic adenocarcinoma with a sensitivity of 80% and lymphoma with a sensitivity of 50% [106]. Interstitial lung disease that is associated with connective tissue disease or inflammatory bowel disease has nonspecific BAL findings.

- [2] Yeager HC, Williams MC, Beckman JF, et al. Sarcoidosis: analysis of cells obtained by bronchial lavage. Am Rev Respir Dis 1976;113:96 – 100.
- [3] Crystal RG, Roberts WG, Hunninghake GW, et al. Pulmonary sarcoidosis: a disease characterized and perpetuated by activated lung T-lymphocytes. Ann Intern Med 1981;94:73 – 94.

The diagnosis of pulmonary congestion due to left heart failure can be supported by the BAL finding of diffuse alveolar hemorrhage. It is not known that cardiovascular drugs can induce diffuse alveolar hemorrhage (except for anticoagulants). Conversely, if the BAL cytology is normal it largely can exclude a drug-induced immunologic reaction (eg, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, organizing pneumonia, eosinophilic lung disease).

items.md: # Bronchoalveolar lavage in the diagnosis of underlying disorders
items.value: Bronchoalveolar lavage in the diagnosis of underlying disorders

items.md: Pulmonary involvement by a malignancy or a systemic disease, such as connective tissue disorders and inflammatory bowel disease, is important. BAL can contribute to the diagnosis of metastatic adenocarcinoma with a sensitivity of 80% and lymphoma with a sensitivity of 50% [106]. Interstitial lung disease that is associated with connective tissue disease or inflammatory bowel disease has nonspecific BAL findings.
